1.synthesis:
The vision opens with a question from a watchman on Zion’s walls: “Who is this coming from Edom, with garments reddened from Bozrah—this one glorious in his attire, striding forward in his great strength?” (Isa 63:1). Edom’s capital Bozrah (modern Buseira in Jordan) represents the historic enemy that exploited Jerusalem’s fall in 586 BCE—blocking fugitives, celebrating the destruction, urging the Babylonians onward (Obad 11–14; Pss 137:7). The divine warrior arriving with blood-stained garments is returning from executing judgment on Israel’s most treacherous neighbor. His self-identification doubles the effect: “It is I, speaking in righteousness, mighty to save” (v. 1).

The second question—“Why are your garments red, as if you had been treading in a winepress?” (v. 2)—establishes the winepress as the central interpretive image. The warrior’s answer is the most comprehensive statement of divine solitary action in Second Isaiah: “I have trodden the winepress alone, and from the nations no one helped me” (v. 3). The grape-juice stains reframed as blood, the nations as the grapes crushed in the press, the divine warrior harvesting judgment from the world’s hostility to YHWH’s covenant people—this is the winepress image that Revelation 14:19–20 will later develop as the most explicit judgment image in the Apocalypse. The day of vengeance was in YHWH’s heart (v. 4)—carried there through the entire period of Israel’s suffering—paired with the year of redemption: the Jubilee-vocabulary of YHWH’s kinsman-redeemer action. The third iteration of the no-human-helper motif (v. 5; cf. Isaiah 59:16): YHWH was appalled at the absence of helpers, so his own arm brought salvation. His fury sustained him through the winepress-treading, and the nations were made drunk with it and their blood poured to the ground (v. 6).
1.historical_context: The winepress image for judgment appears in Joel 3:13 (sickle and winepress for the judgment of the nations) and becomes the governing image for the eschatological judgment in Revelation 14:19–20, which quotes this passage directly. The blood staining the garments “as far as a horse’s bridle” (Rev 14:20) extends the Isaiah image to cosmic scale. The solitary-action motif (v. 3) echoes Isaiah 59:16 and anticipates the Gethsemane narrative: the one who acts for salvation does so without human companionship or help. The Edom-specific setting is historically grounded in the trauma of 586 BCE, but the vision universalizes: the warrior comes from Edom because Edom is the paradigm case of betraying neighbors, not because judgment is limited to Edomites.
1.literary_structure: The Day-of-Vengeance section (vv. 1–6) is structured as a dialogue: two questions from the watchman (who is this? why the red garments?) and two answers from the divine warrior (I am the righteous one; I trod the winepress alone). The dialogue form slows the revelation down for maximum theological impact: the watchman does not immediately recognize what he sees, and the process of asking and answering enacts the gradual disclosure of the divine warrior’s identity and action. The structural placement after chapters 60–62 (the great promises to Zion) and before chapters 63–64 (the lament) creates a chiastic movement: promise, judgment-vision, lament, new creation.
1.theological_application: The divine warrior acting alone (v. 3,5) carries a permanent theological message: when human partnership in the work of justice and redemption is unavailable, YHWH does not require it. His arm is sufficient. The day of vengeance in his heart (v. 4) held through the whole period of Israel’s suffering indicates that the delay of judgment was not divine inactivity but the sustained holding of justice in reserve. Communities shaped by this text develop the capacity to wait for divine justice without either despair (YHWH has forgotten) or inappropriate vengeance (I must act in his place)—because the warrior who has the day of vengeance in his heart will act in the appointed year.
1.christology: The winepress image is applied to Christ’s returning judgment in Revelation 19:15: “He will tread the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God the Almighty.” The garment dipped in blood of the rider on the white horse (Rev 19:13) explicitly echoes the divine warrior’s reddened garments from the winepress treading of Isaiah 63:2–3. The no-human-helper pattern reaches its New Testament apex in Gethsemane and the cross: “all the disciples left him and fled” (Matt 26:56); Jesus, abandoned, completed the act that no human helper could have accomplished. The year of redemption paired with the day of vengeance (v. 4) maps onto the two advents: the first coming inaugurates the year of the Lord’s favor; the second completes the day of vengeance.

7.pericope_label: A Prayer of Praise and Lament
7.synthesis:
The abrupt pivot from the divine warrior’s triumphant winepress-return to the community’s historical confession and lament is structurally intentional: the same God who has just trodden the nations in judgment is the God of Israel’s covenantal history. The prayer begins with the accumulated hasde YHWH—the plural loyal-covenant-acts of YHWH—praised according to all that he has bestowed on the house of Israel (Isa 63:7). YHWH’s initial confidence in Israel at election is recalled: “Surely they are my people, children who will not prove false” (v. 8)—the covenant-establishing declaration that was historically violated. The angel of his presence saved them in the Exodus; YHWH identified himself with Israel’s suffering: “In all their affliction he was afflicted” (v. 9). He lifted them up and carried them through all the days of old as a father carries a child (Deut 1:31).

But Israel rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit (v. 10)—the first explicit reference to grieving the Holy Spirit in Scripture—so YHWH turned against them as their enemy and himself fought against them. The lament then raises the haunting question: “Where is the one who brought them up from the sea? Where is the one who placed his Holy Spirit among them?” (v. 11). The Exodus catalogue continues: leading Moses by the right hand, dividing the waters, guiding through the depths, the Spirit giving rest (vv. 12–14). The petition escalates: “Look down from heaven and see—where are your zeal and your strength? The yearning of your heart and your compassion are withheld from me” (v. 15). The appeal shifts to divine Fatherhood: even if Abraham and Jacob could not recognize the exilic community as their heirs, YHWH still owns the relationship as Father and Redeemer from of old (v. 16). The prayer closes with the theological boldness of the lament: asking YHWH why he permitted the apostasy that led to exile (v. 17), lamenting the trampling of the sanctuary by adversaries, and the exile community’s self-description as equivalent to uncovenant pagans: “We have become like those you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name” (v. 19).
7.historical_context: The first explicit reference to grieving the Holy Spirit in verse 10 is the scriptural root of Ephesians 4:30: “Do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God.” The divine empathy of verse 9—“in all their affliction he was afflicted”—is one of the most striking expressions of divine solidarity in the entire Hebrew Bible. The Exodus history rehearsed in verses 11–14 establishes the pattern the lament appeals to: the God who acted at the Reed Sea and through the wilderness must act again. The sanctuary-trampling of verse 18 refers specifically to the Babylonian destruction of the Jerusalem temple in 586 BCE. The exile community’s self-identification as equivalent to uncovenant peoples in verse 19 is the lament’s sharpest formulation: the covenant identity marker has been functionally erased.
7.literary_structure: The Prayer-of-Praise-and-Lament section (vv. 7–19) moves through the standard lament structure: praise of YHWH’s past acts (vv. 7–9), historical retrospective of Israel’s failure and YHWH’s response (vv. 10–14), direct petition (v. 15), appeal to divine Fatherhood (v. 16), confession and complaint about divine permission of apostasy (v. 17), and final lament over the sanctuary’s desecration and the community’s reduced status (vv. 18–19). The prayer continues into chapter 64 without structural break, making Isaiah 63:7–64:12 the longest sustained lament-prayer in the entire prophetic corpus.
7.theological_application: The grief of the Holy Spirit (v. 10) is the prophetic formulation that reveals something about the nature of the divine-human relationship: YHWH’s Spirit is not indifferent to human covenant-response but can be wounded by it. This does not imply divine weakness but genuine covenant-relationship: the same empathy that made YHWH share Israel’s affliction (v. 9) makes him capable of being grieved by their rebellion. The appeal to divine Fatherhood over biological ancestry in verse 16—even if Abraham and Jacob could not claim us, you are still our Father—is the deepest possible appeal: not to merit or historical record but to the unconditional nature of the Father-child relationship. Communities in crisis learn from this prayer to appeal to YHWH’s character rather than their own performance.
7.christology: The angel of YHWH’s presence (mal’ach panav, v. 9) who saved Israel in the Exodus is the personified divine presence that theological tradition identifies as a theophanic appearance of the pre-incarnate Son. The carrying of Israel like a shepherd carrying the flock through the wilderness (v. 9) is the image Jesus uses for himself in Luke 15:4–5: “he lays it on his shoulders rejoicing.” The grief of the Holy Spirit (v. 10) finds its New Testament echo in Ephesians 4:30: Paul’s direct citation establishes the continuity between the Spirit who was grieved in Israel’s wilderness rebellion and the Spirit who can be grieved by the church’s conduct. The divine Fatherhood of verse 16 is the ground of the Lord’s Prayer: “Our Father in heaven” (Matt 6:9)—the appeal to Fatherhood that Christ taught his disciples to make.
